skill.name: authentication-setup
skill.description: Design authentication and authorization systems with JWT, OAuth, sessions, password hashing, middleware, and role-based access control guidance.
skill.summary: Build secure login, token, OAuth, and RBAC workflows for backend applications.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, environment, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found these are mostly fenced documentation examples, reference links, environment variable templates, and relative documentation links, not executable skill code or malicious instructions. The skill still needs a marketplace warning because it generates security-sensitive authentication code and includes example refresh-token handling that should be hardened before production use.

medium_findings.title: Raw Refresh Token Handling in Example Code
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE semantic risk. The authentication route examples store refresh tokens directly and return refresh tokens in API responses. This is common in tutorials, but production systems should hash stored refresh tokens, rotate them, bind them to device context when appropriate, and document secure cookie alternatives.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced route examples clearly persist and return refresh tokens. The risk is in generated implementation quality rather than malicious marketplace behavior.

medium_findings.title: Security-Sensitive Generated Code Requires Expert Review
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE contextual risk. The skill guides creation of JWT signing, token verification, authentication middleware, and login endpoints. These are legitimate capabilities, but flawed output could create account takeover, token leakage, or authorization bypass issues if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code areas implement core authentication behavior. No malicious intent is visible, but the domain has high security impact and needs human validation.

medium_findings.title: Endpoint Examples Do Not Fully Implement Stated Controls
medium_findings.description: Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW. The skill later requires rate limiting, approved CORS origins, and input validation, but the main route examples focus on basic flows and do not show full validation or login rate limiting. Users may deploy incomplete examples if they do not apply the later constraints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The omission is visible in the endpoint examples, and the missing controls are named later in the same file. The severity depends on whether users treat snippets as production code.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious execution. The static command findings occur inside fenced examples, SQL snippets, TypeScript snippets, or a hardcoded openssl secret-generation command. No helper script or instruction executes arbitrary user input from the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is visibly fenced tutorial content. The only shell command substitution shown uses hardcoded openssl arguments for local secret generation.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Reference Links and Localhost Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for exfiltration. The hardcoded URLs are public documentation and library references, and the localhost URL is a CORS configuration example. No code sends credentials to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited network strings are documentation links or localhost configuration. I found no evidence of hidden callbacks or credential transmission.

low_findings.title: Environment and Filesystem Findings Are Templates
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for credential theft. The environment variable references demonstrate standard secret loading and .env.example output. The relative paths at the end are related-skill documentation links, not path traversal logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is standard configuration guidance and Markdown links. No file-reading function or secret collection workflow is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Scanner Hits Are Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The skill recommends bcrypt, argon2, JWT, OAuth, HTTPS, and strong environment-managed secrets. The scanner appears to flag authentication terminology, not an instruction to use MD5, SHA-1, DES, or another weak algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe authentication features and recommended password-hashing libraries. I found no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm guidance in those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Refresh Tokens Persisted as Plain Tokens
dangerous_patterns.description: The example stores refresh token strings directly in the database. This pattern increases impact if the token table is exposed and should be changed to store a hash of each refresh token.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token persistence is explicit in both registration and login examples. The pattern is risky but appears educational, not malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: JWT and Secret Handling Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes JWT signing and environment secret examples. These are legitimate for authentication setup, but users must provide strong secrets, rotation, issuer checks, audience checks, and production storage controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited snippets directly handle JWT secrets and database URLs. The risk is configuration misuse rather than suspicious behavior by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution in Secret Generation Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows shell command substitution to create random token secrets. This is acceptable because the command is hardcoded, but copied shell examples still require user review in restricted environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution pattern is real, but the arguments are fixed and generate local random secrets. There is no user-controlled command injection evidence.

content.value_statement: Authentication systems are difficult to implement safely and consistently. This skill gives structured guidance for login flows, token management, OAuth, middleware, and RBAC.
actual_capabilities: Designs user, refresh token, and OAuth provider data models.
Provides password hashing and verification guidance with bcrypt or argon2.
Shows JWT access and refresh token generation and verification patterns.
Creates Express-style authentication and role-based authorization middleware examples.
Outlines registration, login, refresh, logout, and current-user API endpoints.
Lists environment variables, security constraints, and common troubleshooting steps.
limitations: Examples focus mainly on Express.js and PostgreSQL patterns.
Generated authentication code needs security review before production use.
OAuth provider details are high level and require provider-specific configuration.
It does not replace a formal threat model or penetration test.

use_cases.title: Add Login to a New API
use_cases.description: Create registration, login, token refresh, and current-user endpoints for a new backend service.

use_cases.title: Plan Role-Based Access Control
use_cases.description: Define user roles, protected middleware, and admin-only route behavior for product features.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Auth Migration
use_cases.description: Move an existing application toward JWT, OAuth, or session-based authentication with clear constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Login Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new backend
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a basic authentication plan for my app. Use JWT, Express.js, PostgreSQL, password hashing, and short-lived access tokens.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Auth Middleware
prompt_templates.prompt: Create authentication middleware and role-based authorization middleware for my backend. Include token validation, error responses, and user context.

prompt_templates.title: Add OAuth Providers
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend my authentication design with Google and GitHub OAuth. Explain required tables, environment variables, callback routes, and account-linking behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this authentication design for production readiness. Check token storage, refresh token rotation, rate limiting, CORS, validation, logging, and secret management.

output_examples.input: I need JWT authentication for an Express API with PostgreSQL.
output: A user and refresh-token schema tailored for PostgreSQL.
Password hashing guidance using bcrypt or argon2.
Access and refresh token flow with expiry guidance.
Middleware and endpoint structure for protected routes.

output_examples.input: I need admin-only routes and normal user routes.
output: Role definitions for users and administrators.
Authorization middleware behavior for protected endpoints.
Expected unauthorized and forbidden response handling.

output_examples.input: My users are often logged out after token refresh.
output: A troubleshooting checklist for refresh token storage and expiry.
Checks for environment variables and signing secret mismatches.
Recommendations for cleanup jobs and token lifecycle review.

best_practices: Review generated authentication code with a security engineer before production deployment.
Store secrets in environment-managed infrastructure and rotate them on a defined schedule.
Add validation, rate limiting, audit logging, and secure cookie controls around login flows.
anti_patterns: Do not copy tutorial authentication snippets directly into production without hardening.
Do not store plaintext passwords, plaintext refresh tokens, or secrets in source control.
Do not put sensitive personal data, passwords, or payment data inside JWT payloads.

faq.question: What authentication methods does this skill support?
faq.answer: It covers JWT, sessions, OAuth 2.0, refresh tokens, password hashing, and role-based access control.

faq.question: Can I use the output in production?
faq.answer: Use it as a starting point. Review, test, and harden the code before production deployment.

faq.question: Does it manage real secrets for me?
faq.answer: No. It shows environment variable patterns, but your infrastructure must store and rotate secrets securely.

faq.question: Does it include MFA guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It can include TOTP-based MFA planning and backup-code guidance when requested.

faq.question: Which frameworks are best supported?
faq.answer: The strongest examples target Express.js with TypeScript and PostgreSQL. Other frameworks need adaptation.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill handles security-critical code. Static alerts were mostly false positives, but generated auth code needs careful review.
